unresolved_choice_destinations: Bloom Festival work is voluntary. Those who do not attend encounter the decorations and rumors later without being treated as absent from the story.
Cassian arrives late, offers no explanation, and stays close enough to help without announcing that intention.
Finn is present but quieter than usual, sketching the room before deciding whether to join the conversation.
First-year safety instruction is mandatory; unsupervised manifestations can alter nearby wards before a student recognizes what they have asked of possibility.
Harvest attendance is expected, not forced. Houses need volunteers, and absences are explained rather than punished.
Lantern Night remains a school tradition, but students may stay in their Houses unless assigned a safety role.
Lyra has made time to be here, though the untouched cup beside her suggests she came from somewhere else in a hurry.
Orion orders the assembly because every dormitory and classroom must know how to recognize and protect the returned students.
Second-year registration is mandatory because returning students must reconnect their names to the Academy wards.
The Frostfire Feast is expected for House representatives; other students may attend, help elsewhere, or remain in their dormitories.
The Winter Bell assembly is compulsory because the wards will treat anyone outside the accounted circles as missing.
The evening welcome in Lethren Fold is optional. Delegates who rest are briefed the next morning.
The first safety assessment is mandatory because even F-rank contracts can expose a team to lethal conditions.
The guild oath is required before a recruit may enter contract areas or be covered by guild protections.
The guild supper is optional; members who skip it receive the contract summary at dawn.
The hearing is mandatory for witnesses because the Bell may repeat testimony through anyone who heard it secondhand.
The judgment hearing requires the involved party to attend, but spectators may leave or submit written testimony.
The opening convocation is mandatory because the gates must account for every arriving student before the wards settle.
The remembrance drill is mandatory because an unanchored name can vanish from several people at once.
The review is mandatory because the guild must record responsibility before another team accepts the route.
The road briefing is expected for survey volunteers; anyone declining must report to ordinary lessons instead.
The spring assembly is required so the changed roads and restored Houses can be explained before classes resume.
Toma trades bread for peaches from a seasonal cart, then spends the quiet hour teaching his son the recipe he once refused to share.
Toma, the baker from the corner stall, argues softly with his teenage son about burnt honey rolls while wrapping the least burnt ones for students who cannot afford to be particular.
Year-end preparation is expected for assigned teams, while unassigned students can choose ordinary classes or community work.
